title: Failed backflow test next steps
description: What a failed backflow test usually means, how repair and retest sequencing works, and where owners lose time.
intro: Most owners do not get into trouble because the device failed. They get into trouble because the repair, retest, and submission sequence was handled sloppily.
callout: The governing utility page stays canonical. This guide exists to make the failure workflow easier to read before you jump back into the local program rules.
sourceExcerpt: A failed test becomes a compliance problem when repair, retest, and submission are not completed in the utility's required sequence and timeline.

sections.heading: Failure rarely ends at one invoice
sections.body: A failed test can trigger repair labor, parts, another visit, retest submission, and in some cities a shortened compliance window.
bullets: Some utilities give a specific repair window, such as 30 days.
An incomplete or rejected report can keep the property noncompliant even after the device is fixed.

sections.heading: Keep the paperwork chain intact
sections.body: Repair completion is not the same thing as compliance completion. The utility or its vendor still needs the right test report in the right system.
bullets: Use the same governing utility workflow that handled the original notice.
If the utility uses BSI or VEPO, make sure the follow-up tester and report path match the system rules.

sources.label: Fort Worth testing and inspection sequence
